title: There is a Mildly Mixing Rank 1 Transformation with an Interesting Joining
speaker: Jonathan Chaika
abstract: Rank 1 transformations are a long studied, flexible class of measure preserving transformations. King proved a variety of deep results about rank 1 rank transformations including: A) A rank 1 transformation’s centralizer is the weak closure of its powers. B) The factors of rank 1 transformations are rigid and thus mildly mixing rank 1 transformations are prime. (Mild mixing means no rigid factors.) C) Mixing rank 1 transformations have minimal self-joinings. Thouvenot asked if mildly mixing rank 1 transformations automatically have minimal self-joinings and the point of this talk is to say that they don’t. Open problems will be presented and no previous knowledge of joinings nor rank 1 systems will be assumed. This is joint work with Donald Robertson.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Main theorem and minimal self-joining setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the main result—there exists a mildly mixing rank-one transformation without minimal self joinings—and defines joinings, self joinings, off-diagonal joinings, and the MSJ property.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for joinings through factors and higher mixing
proof_stages.summary: Joinings are motivated first as objects arising from factor maps and then as a way to formulate obstructions to Rokhlin's question on whether mixing implies mixing of all orders.

proof_stages.label: Mild mixing as absence of rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Mild mixing is defined by the absence of nontrivial rigid functions and then related to equivalent set-return, product-ergodicity, and mixing-sequence characterizations, placing it strictly between weak and strong mixing.

proof_stages.label: Rank-one tower approximation
proof_stages.summary: Rank one is defined via a sequence of Rokhlin towers whose levels approximate every measurable set, and cutting-and-stacking pictures are used to explain how such systems are built.

proof_stages.label: Known rank-one rigidity theory
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why rank-one systems are important—typicality, simple spectrum, restricted centralizers and factors, and known results on mixing rank-one and finite-rank systems imply strong joining and higher-mixing rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Constraints on rank-one pairwise-independent joinings
proof_stages.summary: The possible existence of rank-one systems with pairwise-independent non-product joinings is narrowed by noting such systems cannot be mixing and cannot be rigid, with mixing rank-one systems already known to have minimal self joinings.

proof_stages.label: Thouvenot question and significance of the negative answer
proof_stages.summary: A hypothetical positive answer to Thouvenot's question would combine MSJ, two-simplicity, weak mixing, and the Glasner–Host–Rudolph mechanism to rule out non-product pairwise-independent joinings for rank-one systems, so the announced negative theorem separates mild mixing from the mixing rank-one picture.

proof_stages.label: Construction sketch in questions
proof_stages.summary: In the question period the speaker sketches that the example is built from a variable-base odometer as a first-return rank-one map and carries an explicit two-to-one self joining obtained as a weak-star limit of off-diagonal joinings.

examples.name: Two-symbol Bernoulli shift with pairwise-independent joinings
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that for the two-symbol shift with product measure one can build pairwise-independent non-product joinings, illustrating that the Rokhlin-type obstruction is not hard in positive-entropy symbolic systems but is open in the weak-mixing entropy-zero setting.
examples.engagement_reason: he only says the two-symbol shift with product measure can have such pairwise-independent joinings, without constructing or analyzing one

passes.reason: The binary Bernoulli/XOR-type joining is a maximally regular product/algebraic example used to refute pairwise independence implying product.
the two-symbol Bernoulli/parity joining is a highly regular iid algebraic construction used to show pairwise-independent joinings need not be product
The fair binary Bernoulli/XOR joining is a highly regular product-group construction showing pairwise independence need not force a product joining.
A standard binary Bernoulli-family system whose pairwise-independent nonproduct joining shows that pairwise independence need not imply product in general.
the Bernoulli product shift is a highly regular iid system, and its non-product pairwise-independent joining shows pairwise independence need not force product in general
A regular binary Bernoulli/parity joining is used to show pairwise independence need not imply the product joining.
It is used to show that pairwise-independent joinings need not be product, with the Bernoulli bit construction being a highly regular iid example.
Used to show that pairwise-independent joinings need not be product joinings in general; the Bernoulli/parity construction is a highly regular product model.
used to show pairwise-independent non-product joinings can exist; the binary Bernoulli/parity construction is highly regular
Shows pairwise-independent joinings need not be product using the highly regular binary Bernoulli system
It is used to show non-product pairwise-independent joinings can exist, and the fair binary Bernoulli shift is the max-entropy iid binary system.
